Switch interview mode

3 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.

Curriculum → Data structures and algorithms interview prep → DSA foundations and warm-ups → Linked lists, binary search, and ordering problems

Lesson 2 of 3

Linked lists, binary search, and ordering problems

Move from pointer manipulation into partitioning, ordering, and search-space pruning.

30-45 min DSA flow DSA foundations and warm-ups 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

These questions expose whether you can manage mutable state, midpoint logic, and off-by-one boundaries without getting lost in implementation details.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.2. Practice set Merge Sorted Array · Easy · Sorting and Searching3. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing These questions expose whether you can manage mutable state, midpoint logic, and off-by-one boundaries without getting lost in implementation details. 2. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.3. Practice set Merge Sorted Array · Easy · Sorting and Searching4. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

These questions expose whether you can manage mutable state, midpoint logic, and off-by-one boundaries without getting lost in implementation details.

What to say first

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
2

Study section

Pattern lens

Practice slow-fast pointers, dummy nodes, partitioned search spaces, and the conditions that let you discard half the search region safely.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the core data structure choice before you code.
  • State the target time and space complexity up front.
  • Use one invariant or pointer relationship to guide the implementation.
3

Study section

Practice set

Use this cluster to rehearse the same solving pattern across multiple prompts. Start with Merge Sorted Array, Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array, Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II and keep your explanation focused on why the pattern applies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Merge Sorted Array · Easy · Sorting and Searching
  • Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array · Easy · Array
  • Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II · Easy · Array
  • Rotate Array · Easy · Array
  • Sort Colors · Medium · Sorting and Searching
  • Top K Frequent Elements · Medium · Sorting and Searching
4

Study section

Execution focus

Keep pointer ownership explicit, draw one example list or sorted range, and say exactly why your midpoint or pointer updates cannot loop forever.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
  • Call out the edge case that is most likely to break the first draft.
  • Finish by restating the complexity and one possible follow-up optimization.

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
  • Test at least one edge case and one representative happy path aloud.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into code without naming the dominant pattern or invariant.
  • Optimizing too early instead of getting the first correct version working.
  • Ignoring duplicates, empty inputs, or off-by-one boundaries until the end.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How do you decide whether a binary-search problem is searching an index, a value, or an answer space?
  • What dummy-node setup makes a linked-list problem easier to reason about?
  • Which boundary cases do you test first when the search region is size 0, 1, or 2?

Coding practice

DSA interview workspace

This lab only surfaces runnable DSA prompts from the lesson set and keeps the editor in a single-file interview format.

WASM runtime idle 6 runnable questions

Problem statement

Merge Sorted Array

Easy Sorting and Searching

You are given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, sorted in non-decreasing order, and two integers m and n, representing the number of elements in nums1 and nums2 respectively.

Merge nums1 and nums2 into a single array sorted in non-decreasing order.

The final sorted array should not be returned by the function, but instead be stored inside the array nums1. To accommodate this, nums1 has a length of m + n, where the first m elements denote the elements that should be merged, and the last n elements are set to 0 and should be ignored. nums2 has a length of n.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums1 = [1,2,3,0,0,0], m = 3, nums2 = [2,5,6], n = 3
Output: [1,2,2,3,5,6]
Explanation: The arrays we are merging are [1,2,3] and [2,5,6].
The result of the merge is [1,2,2,3,5,6] with the underlined elements coming from nums1.

Example 2:

Input: nums1 = [1], m = 1, nums2 = [], n = 0
Output: [1]
Explanation: The arrays we are merging are [1] and [].
The result of the merge is [1].

Example 3:

Input: nums1 = [0], m = 0, nums2 = [1], n = 1
Output: [1]
Explanation: The arrays we are merging are [] and [1].
The result of the merge is [1].
Note that because m = 0, there are no elements in nums1. The 0 is only there to ensure the merge result can fit in nums1.

 

Constraints:

  • nums1.length == m + n
  • nums2.length == n
  • 0 <= m, n <= 200
  • 1 <= m + n <= 200
  • -109 <= nums1[i], nums2[j] <= 109

 

Follow up: Can you come up with an algorithm that runs in O(m + n) time?

Hints

  • You can easily solve this problem if you simply think about two elements at a time rather than two arrays. We know that each of the individual arrays is sorted. What we don't know is how they will intertwine. Can we take a local decision and arrive at an optimal solution?
  • If you simply consider one element each at a time from the two arrays and make a decision and proceed accordingly, you will arrive at the optimal solution.

Runtime model

Runs in-browser with the bundled Pyodide WebAssembly runtime.

Ln 1, Col 1 6 lines 24 words 0.2 KB python

Test case

Run result

Run the selected test case to compare your output against the expected result.
